text: The purpose of the present investigation is examination of the effects of various drying techniques on drying characteristics of sour cherry (Prunus cerasus L.) and the resulting quality parameters. The vacuum–microwave drying (VMD) performed with initial power densities of 240, 360 and 480 W was investigated against convective drying (CD) at temperatures of 50, 60 and 70 °C using freeze drying (FD) as the quality reference. Experiments showed that time required to dehydration was distinctly shorter using VMD compared with CD, ranging from hours to less than half an hour. The deterioration of the phenolic compounds, antioxidant activity and color characteristics were increased with an increase in air temperature of CD, as well as with an increase in material temperature in VMD; however, VMD drying yielded much higher values for these parameters compared with CD as well as similar results to those for FD, indicating great potential for preservation of bioactive compounds in the dried sour cherry using VMD. Furthermore, the amount of bioactive compounds preserved in VMD-treated samples was the highest when power density was lowered to 120 W at the end of drying process, thus preventing possible temperature increase. The drying experiments showed improvement in the extractability of some quercetin and kaempferol derivatives. Thus VMD exhibited higher suitability and productivity for production of dried high-quality sour cherry fruits with longer storage life retaining their initial nutricional values.
